title: What are Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Learning?
concept: Few-shot and zero-shot learning allow models to classify images with little to no task-specific training data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When is a cost-optimal decision threshold preferred over a default 0.5 threshold?

options.text: When the real-world costs of False Positives and False Negatives are unequal.
When the dataset has exactly equal class sizes.
When we want to reduce the size of the validation set.

exercises.explain: Correct! Custom thresholds allow businesses to balance unequal costs of different types of errors.

cards.title: Seeing a unicorn
cards.body: If you read a book describing a unicorn as 'a white horse with a single spiraled horn on its forehead', you can instantly recognize one in a picture even if you have never seen a photo of a unicorn before. Zero-shot learning gives AI models this exact reading and matching ability.

cards.title: Data-Efficient Learning
cards.body:
Few-Shot Learning: Training a model to classify new categories using only a few examples (e.g. 1 to 5 images per class).
Zero-Shot Learning: Classifying unseen categories with zero training images, relying instead on auxiliary descriptions (like text or attributes).

cards.title: A textbook description
cards.body: Traditional deep learning is like memorizing 10,000 flashcards of dogs. Few-shot is like looking at a picture of a rare lemur once and being able to find it in other photos. Zero-shot is like reading a description of a creature and spotting it on a safari without ever seeing a photo.

cards.title: Face ID unlock
cards.body: Your smartphone's Face ID is a few-shot system (specifically 1-shot). When you set up the phone, it takes one scan of your face. It doesn't retrain a giant network on your face; it uses a pre-trained comparator model to match new scans to your single saved profile.

cards.title: It is not magic
cards.body: You might think zero-shot models learn without any data at all. In reality, they are pre-trained on massive, multi-modal datasets (millions of image-text pairs from the web). They leverage this huge foundation to understand new concepts.

left.text:
Requires: 1,000s of images per class
Trains model weights from scratch
Fails on rare concepts

right.text:
Requires: 0 to 5 images per class
Uses pre-trained embeddings
Highly adaptable in production

exercises.prompt: What is the defining characteristic of zero-shot learning?
exercises.explain: Zero-shot learning relies on matching image features with semantic clues (like text descriptions) instead of labeled training photos.

options.text: Classifying unseen image categories without any training images, using auxiliary information like text descriptions
Training a model with zero parameters
Having zero error rate on the validation set
Training a model only on black and white images

exercises.prompt: True or false: Smartphone Face ID is a classic example of standard deep learning that retrains a new model every time a user unlocks their phone.
exercises.explain: False. Face ID is a few-shot (one-shot) system that uses a pre-trained feature matcher to compare the current face with the saved key.
One-shot learning refers to training a classification system with exactly one example per class.
exercises.sentence: A few-shot classification task with one training example per class is called ___-shot.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks represents a few-shot learning approach?
exercises.explain: Classifying a new category (virus variant) using a tiny sample size (3 images) is few-shot learning.

options.text: Training a model to identify a new virus variant using only 3 micro-graphs
Training a ResNet on 1.2 million ImageNet photos for 90 epochs
Evaluating model calibration using temperature scaling on a validation set

exercises.prompt: You are building a flower classification app. The client wants to support 500 species, but they only have 2 photos for each species. Should you try standard CNN training, or a few-shot approach, and why?

options.text: Few-shot approach, because 2 images per class is far too little data for standard CNN training to generalize.
Standard CNN training, because 1,000 total images is plenty to train a ResNet from scratch.
Zero-shot approach, because we must delete the photos to save disk space.
Standard training, but only if we use single-label Softmax activation.

exercises.explain: Exactly! 2 images per class represents a '2-shot' scenario. A standard CNN trained from scratch on this would overfit instantly, memorizing the backgrounds. Few-shot systems leverage pre-trained features to learn relationships from tiny samples. Next, let's look at how Siamese networks compare images!
